discussion_comments.comment_body: This is not reliable as the user may have a different config directory defined, such as on dev where it's `~/.config/waveterm-dev`. You should use `wavebase.GetWaveConfigDir` instead.

However, because this is called from the wsh side, which doesn't have the environment variables defined, you should instead call the `wshclient.PathCommand` function, since that will get you whatever the value is in wavesrv

actually, this won't work at all if you're on a remote machine. The logic for zipping the config should be moved to wavesrv and called through the wshserver mechanism, you can look at the other commands to see how we do this. Then, if the user is invoking this command from a remote machine, we should copy the zipped archive to the output path on the remote machine

To copy the zipped archive to the output path on the remote machine, should I use the `filestore.WFS.WriteFile` or something else? I am using `filestore.WFS.WriteFile` but `ls` doesn't show anything on the remote machine. thanks!

I'm working on a more flexible filesystem traversal mechanism right now, targeting end of this week. Once this is implemented, I'll let you know so you can integrate it here. For now, let's hold on this PR
The s3 uris we support are the internal ones supported by `aws s3 ...`. We rely on the user's AWS config to handle things like virtual-hosted buckets via the S3 endpoint configuration

Currently sending the entire chat history every time
This seems to work fine without any issues of latency, and I think we can probably safely have a max history length to avoid crazy long histories, but another optimization is we can send just the newest message that is actually being streamed and then have the client update its array
I think in this case given that the websocket is a shared resource it probably makes sense to add this optimization

we should think about how to timeout this conn/request if something goes wrong or something hangs.  either a hard timeout after a certain amount of time, or a rolling timeout, like if we don't get a new packet for X seconds, then we kill the connection and exit the loop.  the "killer" can run in a different go routine (as ReadMessage can hang).

addressed in doOpenAIStreamCompletion - cmdrunner.go:1526

I added a timeout to the channel, if we don't get a new message sent from the socket read goroutine in enough time, the function sends a timeout error

what do you think about this?
Some issues with this are if the readmessage call hangs, this doesn't kill the socket read goroutine bc it's somewhat difficult to do so, so that process will persist taking up resources. If there's a way to set a timeout to web socket read message, I should do that as well 

Also, it sends a timeout error packet so the client loses all the information it had up until the timeout. I think it might be better to add a packet to the output that looks like [Server timeout] or something, so the client can still see what they got up to that point. That's difficult to do tho, because the openai packets have an index and other stuff. We would have to save the last packet in order to create our own

What do you think? is this okay for this release or should I revisit this?

The way to "unstick" a file/connection read in go is to close the connection.  So once you do websocketConnection.Close() (or whatever the gorilla equivalent is) that should cause the readmessage call to fail.  (be careful about setting two error messages in this case though)

addressed. My solution for this is to pass the web socket connection as a parameter to the channel read function and close the socket when it is done, so the socket always closes. This shouldn't have any issues with double errors because the reader stops reading when it hits its timeout

The gorilla web socket documentation didn't say whether it was ok to call close on a socket twice (in the best case the channel write goroutine and the channel read goroutine both call close one after the other), but I tested it and didn't have any issues
Use DialContext and we should set some sensible timeout value for connecting
